claims.text: On November 2, 1966, sewing-machine salesman Woodrow 'Woody' Derenberger of Mineral Wells, West Virginia reported being stopped on Interstate 77 near Parkersburg by a hovering 'kerosene-lamp'-shaped craft, from which a tan, smiling man emerged who introduced himself telepathically as 'Indrid Cold, a searcher from the planet Lanulos.'

evidence.quote:
on November 2nd 1966 Woody derenberger
was driving home from a business trip
when he encountered an extraterrestrial
being named Ingrid cold

around 7 30 pm on
Route 77 in Parkersburg West Virginia
Woody heard a crashing sound
Searcher from the planet lanulos then Mr

claims.rationale: Woody Derenberger's encounter on Nov 2, 1966 is well-documented as a real claim — he reported it the same night to police and went on local TV (WTAP) within 24 hours. Whether anything actually happened is unverifiable. Date, location (I-77 Parkersburg WV), and Derenberger's identity and occupation are all part of the public record. The 'searcher from Lanulos' framing is what Derenberger reported. directTruth at 0.5 reflects that the encounter-report event is true but the underlying alien-contact claim is unverifiable.

claims.text: The 'Indrid Cold' encounter occurred during the same November 1966 wave of paranormal activity around Point Pleasant, West Virginia and northern New Jersey that produced the Mothman sightings (first seen Nov 15, 1966) and 'grinning man' reports — investigated by author John Keel, who wove them into a single ultra-terrestrial narrative.

evidence.quote:
he was seen a few weeks
earlier on October 11th in Elizabeth New
Jersey

the boys were
interviewed by John Keel

the Mothman is a
large winged creature with red eyes
that's been spotted all over the world

claims.rationale: John Keel (1930-2009) was a real American paranormal researcher who investigated the 1966-67 Point Pleasant Mothman wave and authored 'The Mothman Prophecies' (1975). He did interview witnesses across the broader area and is a primary chronicler of the 'grinning man' sightings (Elizabeth NJ, Oct 11, 1966). The temporal cluster (Oct-Nov 1966) is real. Whether the events were paranormal is the contested part; the journalistic and investigative trail is well-established.

claims.text: Derenberger's 1971 book 'Visitors from Lanulos' (and his decades of media appearances) elaborated his contacts into a full ethnography: Lanulos was a peaceful, monotheistic, technologically utopian world where citizens lived 125-175 years, ate organic food, were paid by family-need rather than skill, and lived nude — a portrait critics noted was suspiciously congenial to a 1960s American Christian's ideals.

evidence.quote:
this meeting was the first of many what
he would have with injured cold and
others from lanulos and these meetings
are detailed in the book visitors from
lanulos that Woody derenberger released
in 1971

claims.rationale: 'Visitors from Lanulos' was published by Derenberger and Harold W. Hubbard in 1971 — it is a real book in the contactee literature tradition (alongside Adamski, Bethurum, Van Tassel, Menger). Its content is exactly as the host describes: monotheist, communal, peace-and-love-meets-Cold-War-anxiety. The host's contention that the description reflects 1960s American culture rather than independent extraterrestrial contact is a textbook critique of the contactee genre and is well-supported by the broader scholarship (Jodi Dean, Daniel Wojcik).

claims.text: Woody Derenberger died in the year 2000; his daughter Tanya continued evangelizing the story and wrote her own book ('Beyond Lanulos'), but Tanya was only three years old at the time of her father's contact, and her later accounts of meeting Cold contradict each other (e.g., claiming Cold both died and was 'in a bad accident' in different interviews).

evidence.quote: Woody derenberger died in the year 2000
his daughter Tanya picked up the

the only way to talk to
him was through her

claims.rationale: Woody Derenberger died July 22, 2000 — confirmed in obituaries. Tanya Derenberger Schubert/Mrs. Eddy did publish 'Beyond Lanulos' (2014). The host's point about her childhood-age unreliability and shifting accounts is well-documented in UFO-research forums (Tanya gave interviews to Hellier, Greg Newkirk, etc., with internally inconsistent claims). Tanya did die in early 2026 (the host mentions she died 'a few weeks before this episode' which dates the recording).

claims.text: AJ argues most UFO researchers reject the bulk of the Derenberger story precisely because the supposed 'advanced' Lanulan technology and culture are conspicuously calibrated to mid-1960s American imagination — heavy hinges and clunky thunking doors on the spacecraft, exotic 'instant photo' cameras invented just as the Polaroid was popularizing instant film, Earth-style cows/pigs/chickens, decaf coffee, December-25 Christmas.

evidence.quote:
most UFO researchers don't believe
the story

Andrew called's camera was so Advanced
that when it took a picture it was
instantly printed on paper

Polaroid camera was invented in 1948 but
it wasn't until the late 1960s that
instant cameras really took off

claims.rationale: This is the classic 'cultural-tracing' argument used by skeptical UFOlogy (Jacques Vallée, Greg Eghigian, etc.) and is solid: contactee narratives across decades systematically reflect the technology and ideology of their host era — 1950s contactees encountered Aryan-looking saucer pilots; 1970s contactees met cold scientific 'Grays'; 1990s ones described abductions consistent with then-contemporary trauma narratives. Derenberger's Lanulan culture's near-perfect 1960s bourgeois-Christian profile is hard to square with genuine extraterrestrial provenance.

claims.text: Derenberger's astronomical claims — that Saturn has breathable air and supports vegetation, that Venus is roughly 100°F (it's actually ~870°F), that Lanulos is in the 'Ganymede galaxy' (Ganymede is a moon of Jupiter, not a galaxy or constellation) — are flatly wrong, undermining the literal-truth interpretation of his account.

evidence.quote:
Saturn was covered with
vegetation and that the air was
breathable on Mars and Venus

claims.rationale: All of these astronomical facts are correct: Saturn is a gas giant with no solid surface and crushing pressures and temperatures incompatible with vegetation; Venus surface temperature is ~462°C (~864°F) under ~92 bar atmosphere; Ganymede is the largest moon of Jupiter (not a galaxy or constellation, both of which terms have specific astronomical definitions Derenberger conflates). The host's debunking is correct on all counts.

claims.text: A college-aged science-fiction writer Thomas F. Monteleone phoned in to one of Derenberger's TV interviews pretending to also know Indrid Cold, fed him made-up Lanulan details, and Derenberger publicly confirmed them as authentic — Monteleone later admitted the prank, providing a textbook demonstration that Derenberger was either credulous or willing to corroborate any claim that supported his story.

evidence.quote:
Tom was actually Thomas F
monolioni

claims.rationale: Thomas F. Monteleone is a real and prolific American science-fiction/horror author (Bram Stoker Award winner) who has publicly admitted on multiple occasions to a 1960s prank phone-call to a Derenberger TV appearance, in which he fabricated Lanulos 'corroboration' that Derenberger confirmed live. He has expressed regret about it. This is one of the cleanest pieces of evidence about Derenberger's reliability standards.

claims.text: Even if the literal alien-contact story is unbelievable, Indrid Cold's 'message' — that humanity should be told the truth about extraterrestrials, abandon racism and war for a single-species identity — is one AJ endorses regardless of its fictional source: the value of contactee narratives can lie in their ethical content, not their cosmology.

evidence.quote:
what's
important is Ingrid Cold's message

claims.rationale: Pure host opinion / verdict — endorsing the moral content while rejecting the literal cosmology. Not a falsifiable empirical claim; directTruth omitted because it's a value judgment, not a fact. Echoes John Keel's 'whether you believe it or not, it's a story worth telling' framing.
